title: Painting an interior brick wall
subtitle: Prepare a bare or previously painted brick wall and apply a durable masonry-grade interior finish
excerpt: How to paint an interior brick wall so the brick texture reads through the finish without absorbing the topcoat unevenly. Covers cleaning, repointing checks, sealing the porous surface, and the brush and roller order that gets paint into the mortar joints.
sourceNotes: Collins Complete DIY Manual. Reader's Digest Complete DIY Manual.

glossaryTerms.definition: A penetrating sealer for porous masonry that binds loose surface dust and reduces the topcoat's suction into the brick. Applied as a thin first coat under masonry paint.
A 18 mm to 22 mm pile roller sleeve that pushes paint into the recessed mortar joints between bricks. A standard medium-pile sleeve bridges the joints and leaves them under-coated.

content.text: An interior brick wall paints as a feature wall in a hallway, alcove, or fireplace recess. The job is straightforward but the brick demands two adjustments to standard wall painting: a
to push paint into the joints.

Probe each mortar joint with a screwdriver tip. Sound mortar resists; crumbling mortar gives way and falls out as powder. A wall with more than a small area of failing joints needs repointing before painting; paint will not bond to loose mortar and the repair becomes a major job. If the joints are sound, brush the wall down with a stiff hand wire brush to remove loose grit and old paint flakes.

Wash with sugar soap in warm water. Brick holds soot, kitchen grease, and tobacco residue in the porous face, and any of those will reject paint. Scrub with a stiff nylon brush, working into every joint, then rinse twice with clean water. Allow to dry fully: bare brick takes a full 24 hours to lose surface moisture, longer in winter.

Pour stabilising primer into a roller tray. Cut in around the wall edges with the 50 mm brush, working the primer into the perimeter joints. Roll the field with a long-pile roller, pressing the primer into the brick face. The primer dries clear or slightly off-white. Allow the manufacturer's recoat time, typically four to six hours for water-based products.

The first topcoat starts at the joints with the 25 mm brush. Push paint into every horizontal and vertical mortar joint along the whole wall. This step is slow but it determines whether the finish reads as a painted brick wall or a flat-but-lumpy wall. Working into the joints before rolling sets a base in the recesses that the roller cannot reach.

Switch to the 9-inch roller with the long-pile sleeve. Roll the brick faces with overlapping vertical strokes, applying steady pressure. The pile carries paint over the brick faces and helps work the surrounding joint edges. Reload the roller as soon as it skips or laps.

Allow the first coat to dry fully, then repeat the brush-the-joints then roll-the-faces sequence for the second coat. Most walls need two full coats over the primer to reach an even, opaque finish. Inspect under raked light from a side window or a portable lamp before deciding whether a third coat is needed; the side light shows the patchy joints a flat front light hides.

A lime wash is the right finish for an exposed brick wall in a listed home. The method is similar but the wash needs three or four very thin coats. Dampen the brick lightly with clean water just before each coat. Modern masonry paint will not stick to a limewashed wall. Limewash will not stick to a modern emulsion-painted wall. Once a wall is coated in one system, it stays in that system unless fully stripped.

A painted interior brick wall holds for five to eight years before any maintenance. Touch up small chips or scuffs with the original paint kept in a sealed tin. If the wall is in a chimney recess and used with a working fire, expect smoke staining to need a refresh sooner.

attrs.symptom: Patchy finish with mortar joints showing through after two coats
attrs.cause: The joints were rolled rather than brushed first, leaving an uneven film thickness across the wall.
attrs.fix: Brush a third coat into every joint with the 25 mm brush, then roll a fourth full coat over the faces. Allow each coat to dry fully.

attrs.symptom: Paint peels off in sheets within a few months
attrs.cause: The wall held surface moisture or dust when the primer went on, or no stabilising primer was used over a previously painted layer.
attrs.fix: Strip back to bare brick on the failed area, allow to dry fully (a fan and open window for 48 hours), apply stabilising primer, then recoat as steps 4 to 6 above.
